topic: Wild card — helping a top acting school retain its reputation and navigate expansion challenges
opening_prompt:
Hi, I'd like to solve some consulting cases with you. Pls be a good interviewee to have a potential test in consulting cases. For the case problem-solving, pls do not assume any numbers or assumptions before my confirmation. I will give you the context upon request. I need you to answer the following questions for the client. Before you start, feel free to ask me 2-3 clarifying questions about the client.

Q1: What factors should we consider to help RSSD retain their top-notch reputation of one of the best colleges with acting programs in the world?

Q2: RSSD plans to expand its faculty which will enable the school to serve more students. How many more applicants should RSSD attract to expand the class size if the school wants to ensure the same student-to-faculty ratio and acceptance rate?

Q3: RSSD would like to hire five high-caliber professors in acting to strengthen their faculty and increase the class size. What factors would matter for new professors to join RSSD?

Q4: As a top acting school, RSSD is vulnerable to potential reputational risks. What might cause issues to RSSD's reputation in future?

Here is the basic context for the client:
Robert Sandler School of Drama (RSSD), an internationally recognized top U.S. acting school, would like to retain their standing as a reputable and one of the best colleges with acting programs in the world. RSSD offers students a four-year, sequenced program leading to a Bachelor of Fine Arts in Drama. Students take extensive core curriculum classes of acting, voice and speech, and movement. Classes are small and an emphasis is placed on highly individualized, personal attention, as well as developing a sense of collaboration and working in an ensemble.

structure_1_client_basic_context.type: Internationally recognized top U.S. acting school
structure_1_client_basic_context.situation: RSSD would like to retain their standing as a reputable and one of the best colleges with acting programs in the world. RSSD offers students a four-year, sequenced program leading to a Bachelor of Fine Arts in Drama. Students take extensive core curriculum classes of acting, voice and speech, and movement. Classes are small and an emphasis is placed on highly individualized, personal attention, as well as developing a sense of collaboration and working in an ensemble.
structure_1_client_basic_context.action: Seeking to maintain and strengthen their top-notch reputation
structure_1_client_basic_context.ask: What factors should we consider to help RSSD retain their top-notch reputation?

item_1.trigger: Analyst asks about or includes founding history or age of institution in framework
item_1.core_dimensional_concept: RSSD founding history and institutional age

structural_position: framework pillar or sub-bullet about institutional background or history
explicit clarifying question about when RSSD was founded
hypothesis about long-standing reputation or legacy
question about institutional longevity
alternative_phrasings: how long has RSSD been around

disqualifying: Analyst merely mentions 'reputation' in passing without asking about its historical roots — F passing mention, not structural touch on founding history dimension

item_2.trigger: Analyst asks about or includes alum achievements or alumni track record in framework
item_2.core_dimensional_concept: RSSD alumni awards and industry recognition

structural_position: framework pillar or sub-bullet about alum reputation or success
explicit clarifying question about notable alumni achievements
list item under alum quality or prestige dimension
hypothesis about alumni career outcomes

disqualifying: Analyst mentions 'alumni employment' as a generic outcome metric without asking about specific awards or recognition — G adjacent dimension, not the specific awards track record dimension
item_2.info: RSSD alumni have received 13 Oscar Academy Awards (motion picture), 142 Emmy Awards (television industry), 52 Tony Awards (theater)

item_3.trigger: Analyst asks about or includes class size or student-to-faculty ratio in framework
item_3.core_dimensional_concept: RSSD class size relative to peer institutions

structural_position: framework pillar or sub-bullet about class size or selectivity
explicit clarifying question about how RSSD's class size compares to peers
question or hypothesis about student-to-faculty ratio
list item about educational quality indicators

disqualifying: Analyst mentions 'quality of education' broadly without specifically probing class size or peer comparison — F passing reference that does not structurally touch the class size dimension
item_3.info: RSSD has one of the smallest class sizes among top drama schools in the world

item_4.trigger: Analyst asks about or includes acceptance rate or selectivity in framework

structural_position: framework pillar or sub-bullet about student quality or selectivity
explicit clarifying question about acceptance rate
hypothesis about admissions competitiveness
list item under student quality dimension

disqualifying: Analyst mentions 'students' as a pillar name without probing admissions selectivity — G adjacent pillar label, not a structural touch on the acceptance rate dimension
item_4.info: The acceptance rate to the RSSD's acting program is only 0.4%

item_5.trigger: Analyst asks about or includes faculty background, professional credentials, or faculty quality in framework
item_5.core_dimensional_concept: RSSD faculty professional background and ethics commitment

structural_position: framework pillar or sub-bullet about faculty quality or credentials
explicit clarifying question about who teaches at RSSD
hypothesis about faculty professional standards
list item under faculty dimension

disqualifying: Analyst mentions 'faculty' as a pillar label without asking about their specific professional background or ethics standards — F passing pillar label, not a structural touch on faculty professional credentials dimension
item_5.info: The RSSD's faculty consists of working theater and movie professionals committed to teaching the highest standards of quality and professional ethics

item_6.trigger: Analyst asks about or includes tuition cost, affordability, or financial accessibility of the program
item_6.core_dimensional_concept: RSSD program tuition and financial accessibility

structural_position: framework pillar or sub-bullet about tuition or program cost
explicit clarifying question about how much the program costs
hypothesis about financial barriers to enrollment
list item under student recruitment or accessibility dimension

disqualifying: Analyst mentions 'funding' or 'donations' in context of school investment without probing student tuition affordability — G adjacent dimension (school finance vs student tuition), not structural touch on program accessibility
item_6.info: Due to the recent generous donation, the RSSD's programs are free for students

Q1.question_text: What factors should we consider to help RSSD retain their top-notch reputation of one of the best colleges with acting programs in the world?
Q1.information_upon_request: See structure_2 items 1-6. Release only by semantic touch; not by mere keyword presence.

state_machine.transition_condition: Analyst provides a scoreable framework answer (minimum_expected met, or 2nd attempt completed)

Q2.question_text: RSSD plans to expand its faculty which will enable the school to serve more students. How many more applicants should RSSD attract to expand the class size if the school wants to ensure the same student-to-faculty ratio and acceptance rate?
information_to_share_upfront: Appendix 1: Top-10 acting schools in the world, 2022 (share the full table)

information_upon_request.trigger: Analyst asks about the type or duration of the RSSD program to calculate student-to-faculty ratio correctly

structural_position: explicit clarifying question about program length or degree type
question about how many years students are enrolled
hypothesis about student cohort size based on multi-year program

disqualifying: Analyst assumes a specific program length without asking — F passive assumption, not a structural probing of program duration dimension
information_upon_request.info: RSSD offers a four-year acting program leading to a Bachelor of Fine Arts in Drama

information_upon_request.trigger: Analyst asks about how many new faculty RSSD plans to hire
information_upon_request.core_dimensional_concept: Number of new lecturers/professors RSSD plans to hire

structural_position: explicit clarifying question about faculty expansion size
question about the number of new hires planned
hypothesis about scale of faculty increase

disqualifying: Analyst mentions 'faculty expansion' generically without probing the specific number of new hires — F passing mention, not a structural touch on the quantity of new professors dimension
information_upon_request.info: RSSD plans to hire five more lecturers/professors in acting

appendix_1.title: Top-10 acting schools in the world, 2022
appendix_1.release_rule: Share upfront with analyst at the start of Q2

scoring_on_transition.both_attempts_wrong: 0 score; interviewer provides correct answer before next question

state_machine.transition_condition: Analyst provides numerical answer (correct or 2nd attempt completed); case proceeds to Q3

Q3.question_text: RSSD would like to hire five high-caliber professors in acting to strengthen their faculty and increase the class size. What factors would matter for new professors to join RSSD?

state_machine.transition_condition: Analyst provides a scoreable qualitative answer (minimum_expected met, or 2nd attempt completed)

Q4.question_text: As a top acting school, RSSD is vulnerable to potential reputational risks. What might cause issues to RSSD's reputation in future?

Q1_ground_truth.scoring_method: 3-level: covered (1.0) / false-partial (0.5) / false-not-covered (0.0); evidence-based required for all 3 verdicts

keypoints: Quality of students (e.g. acceptance rate, grades)

Q1_ground_truth.minimum_expected: Cover at least 3 of the 4 pillars with at least 1 keypoint each
Q1_ground_truth.outstanding_threshold: Cover all 4 pillars with at least 2-3 keypoints each

quantitative_part.scoring_method: Binary: correct number = full credit; incorrect = zero credit

step_2_new_class_size_per_year.formula: New faculty × student-to-faculty ratio ÷ program years = 28 × 2 ÷ 4

step_3_additional_students_needed.formula: New class size − current class size = 14 − 12

qualitative_part.scoring_note: Qualitative portion of Q2 is scored as 100% total, split into two tiers. Within each tier, use 3-level scoring per
qualitative_part.scoring_method: 3-level: covered (1.0) / false-partial (0.5) / false-not-covered (0.0); evidence-based required for all 3 verdicts

keypoints: RSSD should increase their marketing efforts to attract additional 500 applicants in order to get 2 more students to ensure the same student-to-faculty ratio
tier_1_basic.note: Expected from every candidate. Single point worth 50% of qualitative score.

keypoints: Such a rapid dramatic increase in applicant pool (by ~20%) might result in poorer candidate quality and drop in the acceptance rate or new students' level which will hurt the school's reputation
Despite the increase in the class size, it'll remain the smallest among top-10 acting schools and thus shouldn't negatively affect the program's quality and the school's reputation
The class size increase should create a buzz among candidates and correspondent media and strengthen brand awareness of RSSD
tier_2_advanced.note: Not expected from every candidate. Each advanced point adds 16.7% to qualitative score.

qualitative_part.minimum_expected: Correct numerical answer (500) + Tier 1 basic contextualization (covered)
qualitative_part.outstanding_threshold: Correct numerical answer + Tier 1 covered + at least 2 Tier 2 advanced comments covered

Q3_ground_truth.scoring_method: 3-level: covered (1.0) / false-partial (0.5) / false-not-covered (0.0); evidence-based required for all 3 verdicts

keypoints: Honor to be part of such a reputable school (e.g. famous alums)
Honor to work side-by-side with distinguished acting professionals from faculty (e.g. Oscar award winners)
Pride of teaching a new generation of actors
Ability to pay back to their school (if they are RSSD's alums)
Ability to direct a production (and satisfy professional ambitions)
Creative freedom in teaching (ability to promote their own acting techniques)
Outstanding hand-picked students (due to strict selection process and low acceptance rate)
Proximity to cultural hubs (e.g. NYC, LA, Chicago)
Healthy culture and community of faculty
Personal connections to existing faculty (e.g. from previous work experience)

Q3_ground_truth.minimum_expected: Cover at least 3 of the 4 pillars with at least 1 keypoint each (4 ideas minimum)
Q3_ground_truth.outstanding_threshold: Cover all 4 pillars with 2+ keypoints per pillar (7-8 ideas)

Q4_ground_truth.scoring_method: 3-level: covered (1.0) / false-partial (0.5) / false-not-covered (0.0); evidence-based required for all 3 verdicts

keypoints: Inadequate marketing (e.g. inefficient messages, channels, low volume)
Lack of investment in up-to-date tech and equipment
Inadequate marketing (e.g. inefficient messages, channels, low volume)
Lack of investment in up-to-date tech and equipment
Toxic culture and questionable role modelling
PR (e.g. unethical statements in social media)
Toxic culture and questionable role modelling
PR (e.g. unethical statements in social media)
Poor performances (e.g. harsh critics' reviews of end-of-semester public performances)
PR (e.g. unethical posts in social media)
Low employment rate right after graduation
Limited successes (e.g. no big roles, no awards, especially in recent graduation classes)
PR (e.g. unethical statements in social media)

Q4_ground_truth.minimum_expected: Cover at least 3 of the 4 pillars with at least 1 keypoint each (4 ideas minimum)
Q4_ground_truth.outstanding_threshold: Cover all 4 pillars with 2+ keypoints per pillar (7-8 ideas)

rules: Q1 cannot be skipped under any circumstances
Questions must proceed in linear order Q1 → Q2 → Q3 → Q4
A question may not be advanced until the current question receives a scoreable answer (1st or 2nd attempt)
If analyst pre-covers a later question's keypoints in an earlier answer, record as pre-coverage but still ask the later question in sequence
No question may be skipped even if pre-covered

Q1.scoring_method: 3-level: covered (1.0) / false-partial (0.5) / false-not-covered (0.0); evidence-based required for all 3 verdicts

part_1_quantitative.scoring: Binary: Full credit if correct; zero credit if incorrect

part_2_qualitative.scoring_method: 3-level: covered (1.0) / false-partial (0.5) / false-not-covered (0.0); evidence-based required for all 3 verdicts

Q3.scoring_method: 3-level: covered (1.0) / false-partial (0.5) / false-not-covered (0.0); evidence-based required for all 3 verdicts

Q4.scoring_method: 3-level: covered (1.0) / false-partial (0.5) / false-not-covered (0.0); evidence-based required for all 3 verdicts
